title: Sarah Paine - Why Putin and Xi can't escape geography

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Good evening. It's such a pleasure to be with you. I'm going to make an argument today about how the world is organized, or at least how different types of empires back in the day tried to organize it. It turns out that continental and maritime powers tried to organize the world in very different ways, and that these have real implications in our own day.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And I'm going to be using terminology from geopolitics and also grand strategy. What is that? Geopolitics is the influence of geography on politics, and grand strategy is grand in the number of instruments of national power that it integrates.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And you think, well, what are they? And think about who sits on a cabinet around a president, who gets convened. Those are the elements of national power, right? So you've got the State Department, diplomacy, the military, you've got finance, agriculture, you've got a whole long list of people and you want to integrate these things.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But before I get going, I'm going to give you a sneak detour, a really whirlwind tour of American history which I'll get done really fast, along with a tour of some of the great geopoliticians, and before I will—after that appetizer, I will deliver the promised main course.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Okay. This is June 6th, 1944, D-Day, Normandy landings, where sea power, maritime power meets land power. It turns out maritime powers are the exception and continental powers the rule.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Why? Because maritime powers, if need be, can defend themselves primarily at sea with their navies, whereas a continental power simply cannot—think Ukraine, a navy's not going to save them from Russia.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So this is the first distinguishing characteristic between a maritime and continental power, whether it is feasible to defend yourself at sea or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And from this come some implications, that if you're in a continental world, you'd better have a competent army, and if you're a maritime power to defend yourself, you'd better have a pretty good navy.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But these implications go beyond the military to economics and politics as well, and I'll go into these.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But this country began its life as a continental power, all about expanding to, guess what, here, right, to the West Coast.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And the United States tried invading Canada a couple of times, not a new idea, in 1775 and 1812. The British won those things and we had to back off on that idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then you get here, President Monroe declaring the Monroe Doctrine, which is a classic continental spheres of influence, stay out of my backyard moment. The only trick was that the United States was so weak in those days, telling the Europeans to stay out of the Americas was just on the wish list, it was impossible to deal with. But if you look at the United States, how it became the United States, it's all about territorial expansion

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: expanded to reach the West Coast and unlike a typical continental power which does this with massed armies, the United States preferred checkbook diplomacy, so when Napoleon Bonaparte was short of cash in 1803, that's when the Louisiana purchase is made, and then when the Russians were short of cash, a perennial problem, that's when the United States bought not only Alaska but the northern part of the West Coast.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But when the checks were not accepted, which is what happened with Mexico, the United States did the standard massed armies approach in the Mexican-American War and that's how the United States gets Texas, so that the next check offered the Mexicans accept and that's the Gadsden purchase which gets the United States Tucson, Arizona and other places.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: The longest counterinsurgency in this country's history was against the native population that put up a really bitter struggle. So the longest counterinsurgency is not the recent stuff in the Middle East, it's about what we did to the native population here that eventually had to give up.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And Americans had a name for their national expansion, it was Manifest Destiny, the idea that it was obvious fate of America or the United States to control a good part of North America. And they were all about territorial expansion and this painting which hangs in the, I think it's the western staircase as you're getting into the chamber of the House of Representatives, it goes by the politically correct title nowadays of Westward Ho. Well, that's not the title when it was painted, it was Westward the Course of Empire takes its way. Americans were all about expansion and proud of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: However, a different view starts emerging in the late 19th century proponents... was Captain then later Admiral Alfred Thayer Mahan, who is by far the most famous professor ever associated with the Naval War College where I spent 24 years of my career.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And he's looking at it and goes, actually power and position in the world really isn't a function so much of continental expansion and consolidation, but really it's about all the wealth you can accrue from trade. And he thought there were certain prerequisites to follow a maritime security paradigm.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: What are they? One, you need a moat. You cannot be subject to invasion right across your border or it's over. Secondly, you need a dense internal transportation grid in order to get the goods out in peacetime, you need reliable egress by sea to get the Navy out in wartime, and you need a dense coastal population. These are the people who are going to be running all the commerce.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And you also need stable government institutions that are going to be stable in the sense that they're going to fund a Navy and that they're going to pass laws that are going to make commerce happen. If you look at these prerequisites and you look at China and Russia today, neither one has the full list, far from it. Neither one has a moat.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Both of them have more neighbors than any two other countries on the planet, and many of those neighbors don't like them at all for excellent reasons.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Russia has a lamentable internal transportation grid, whereas China's has been improving.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Neither one has reliable egress by sea, surrounded as they are by narrow, in the case of China, island-clustered seas with a whole—and Russia as well—loads of neighbors.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: These places tend to get blockaded and closed down in wartime.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Russia has reliable egress up north. Well great, no one lives there. What do you want to do? Go pet a polar bear? It will not work out.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In addition, on dense coastal population, yes, China has dense coastal population, but again, it's on a narrow sea. Russia does not have a dense coastal population up north.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And Russia's never had a commerce-driven economy; China much more so under Deng Xiaoping, but recently Xi Jinping has been privileging the crony sector over the private sector.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And neither one has stable government institutions. What's the litmus test for that one?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: That would be transparent regular elections, and dictatorship for life does not remotely qualify.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So China and Russia certainly have maritime ambitions, but I'm not clear they actually understand what goes into a full maritime paradigm anyway.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Anyway, there was a counter-argument to Mahan that comes a generation later by a Briton, Sir Halford Mackinder, who's looking at the world and looking at Britain's problems, and he thinks that actually Russia occupies the best geopolitical location on the world, not Great Britain because—

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: and this is a map from his 1904 article, and he defines as the pivot area—Eurasia is the part of the world he's focused on, and it's where Russia is.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And he's also calls it the great heartland, and he said, look, that area, the pivot area, is the citadel of land power on the great mainland of the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: It is the greatest natural fortress on the world. Why? Because it's insulated by mountains, deserts, frozen seas, and rivers that exit onto the oceans in very inaccessible places during wartime.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And therefore it is impervious to sea power, that Britain can't really get at this area.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And he said, but if you occupy the Heartland, this pivot area, you have the possibility for defense in depth, strategic retreat, and self-sufficiency.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So the Heartland is not insulated by the sea; it's insulated from the sea.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And a maritime power like Britain, if it wants to influence the heartland, it's got to do it on the periphery.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: In those days, it would be through having colonies in these very strategic locations. In our own day, it would be having allies with bases in these locations.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: This man, Nicholas Spykman, was also very concerned about who controls Eurasia. He felt the country that controls Eurasia could well control the world. And he had much to worry about when he completed his very important work in 1943, the year he dies. He's a naturalized American citizen from the Netherlands, then under Nazi occupation. And he felt that the Nazis had come really close to dominating Eurasia in 1941. And here's what he said:

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: "Despite occupying the safest position of any nation in the world, we Americans have been involved in two devastating world wars in the space of a quarter of a century. And in the second one, i.e., the ongoing one, we were at one point at in serious danger of defeat. The expectations of U.S. statesmen regarding the outcome of their actions were consistently wrong, and their thinking about national security generally failed to provide successful answers."

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Sound familiar? In addition, he's looking at the world, he said, "Look, the influence of the United States can be brought to bear in Europe and Far East only by means of seaborne traffic. And the power of the states of Eurasia can reach us effectively only by crossing the seas. And this is true despite the development of air power because big things go by sea, and they still do."

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Therefore, U.S. security was a function of sea power, and that sanctuary at home—and by that I mean invulnerability to attack—is a function of a maritime shield. And Spykman's really concerned who controls the rimland of this great heartland, friend or foe. And here's why:

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: "The United States will have to depend on her sea power communication across the Atlantic and the Pacific to give her access to the old world. And the effect of this, of this act, access, is going to delimit what sort of foreign policy the United States can have. And what the United States requires is a continental ally who can provide a base from which land power can be exercised."

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: What he's saying in World War II, victory in that thing is going to depend on alliances and allies. Critical.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right. So that was the hors d'oeuvre. I've done the whirlwind tour of American history, settled in a few minutes or less—you should be happy about that one. And then I've given you some of the most famous geopoliticians there are, a synopsis of what their ideas are. But here's my—this is the main course.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: First I'm going to talk about continental empires because they come first in human history, and then I'll turn to maritime empires which come later. But then there's the Industrial Revolution and the post-World War II institutional framework that opens the possibility of a maritime global order. So that's my game plan.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Oh, I forgot one thing. I don't know whether you all like animals...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Whether they're symbols or not, but we at the Naval War College love animals. And so we often refer to continental powers as elephants and maritime powers as whales. Bonus terminology.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, I'm going to start out with China as—not Russia, which Mackinder was preoccupied with—as the original land power. And here is Sun Tzu, the preeminent theorist, Chinese theorist on matters of strategy and his Art of War.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And Sun Tzu’s providing advice to kings on how to stay on the throne, to conquer neighbors and do it efficiently. And in this world, you've got lots of neighbors, any of which can invade at any time. Third-party intervention is the norm in this world.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And if you think about this book, it makes no references to maritime warfare at all and it makes only tangential references to riverine warfare. And you go, well, why is that? Well, it's for excellent reasons.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: He is writing about the world of continental empires in existence long before the maritime things get going. And if you think about the great civilizations of Eurasia, often at their basis is their great continental empires. And a civilization answers the questions: How should I live? and How should I interact with others? and is basically a world order unto itself. And civilizations have often fought with each other to make their rules universal.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, whirlwind tour of China and what it looks like to be a continental empire. So here's a precipitation map. Here's where it doesn't rain very much. Here's temperatures, that's where it's awfully cold. Here's topography, that's where it's flat. So if you notice, if you want to grow things, you need it where it's warm enough, it rains enough, etc. And here's a really good topographical map. And it gives you an understanding of why famine afflicted China for so many centuries of its history and why it's dependent on food imports today. It's just too much of the real estate's vertical to grow things on.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then here's an ethnic map. You can see the consolidated pink area, that's where the Han, the preponderant ethnic group of China, live. And then they're surrounded with all these heterogeneous areas around them. You can go, 'Hmm, I wonder what's going on there.'

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then here's a simplified ethnic map. It shows China proper and the curious might ask, how did the Han wind up with all the prime real estate? Because what's arable is there.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And the answer would be, well, they laid waste to the competing Dzungar and Tibet empires. I bet you've never heard of the Dzungars. They were wiped out by the Qing dynasty a long time ago. And in the continental world, you're faced with a binary choice: you either become Han or they will kill you. And so you've got to get out of Dodge and flee south or do something else. And genocide is what happens to the losers in continental warfare. And apparently in our—

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: One day, the Uyghurs are slated for genocide.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, I'm going to give you a whirlwind tour, sanitized version of Chinese history. The Hans start up up north in the Yellow River Valley and they spread and they spread and they do fun stuff like they build walls, they build more walls, not a new idea. And then they shrink back and oh, that bad, bad time. But then they're back. And then oh, I don't know what happened there, but anyway, they oh, they consolidated and then there's this thing, which looks like any other dynasty except a little bigger. Uh, not so. The Yuan Dynasty are Mongols. They're not Han. This is an occupation dynasty and that map's inaccurate.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Because here's the real story. The Mongols start out east of Lake Baikal and then they spread in concentric rings more or less over the Eurasian plain in another time of climate change when their herds, they just had to move south or everyone's going to freeze to death. The Chinese act like it's like any other dynasty. No, it's not. It's the Pax Mongolica, it's the Mongol, uh, world order when the Han were subjugated people. But they come back with the Ming Dynasty.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But then the Mings succumb to the second largest empire in Chinese history. Another conquest dynasty. These folks aren't Han, they're Manchu. And what's fascinating is today when China claims whatever its historic lands are, they either choose the Mongol Yuan Dynasty or the Manchu Qing Dynasty when the Han were subjugated people. So try to untangle that logic. I'm not even going to go there. Um, but the point was not to make that snide remark, although I did make that snide remark, uh, but rather to show you that there's lots and lots of expansion and lots and lots of shrinkage and lots and lots of bloodshed while this is taking place. China has been raising armies denominated in hundreds of thousands of people for thousands of years. The West does not do this, at least not on a regular basis until the Napoleonic Wars. Uh, it's a brutal world.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Now Mackinder didn't much care about China one way or the other because in his lifetime, uh, China was a country in freefall. So he's focusing on Russia, the continental empire. Mackinder's really concerned about Russia also because it's building railways that are going to give it all these internal lines of communication and Mackinder thinks that's going to work really well for them in wartime, whereas Britain is stuck with exterior lines of communication, exterior because you're going by sea and the, uh, on the periphery of things.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So if you look at the Russians, they're like Mongols in reverse. They start in Moscow and then Moscow lays waste to the other city, princely city-states and after that they're after furs making money that way. And the Russians keep going until they run into countries that fight back.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So, to show you how this works, this is the Russian Empire at its height in 1914. So you can see Moscow at the historic capital and you can look at the colors there and see, oh, the administrative units are fairly small around Moscow and they get really big as you go out into Siberia. And these are civil administrative units.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And here's a clearer map except it's not really clear. The bigger these units, they're provinces, they're called guberniya in Russian or governorates. But anyway, these provinces are standard administrative units. But for the recently ingested and yet to be digested, they're put into different units called oblast, oblasti. And you can't see them on map, they're on gray print. So this shows you more or less where they're located, outlined in red is where these places are. These are the places in process of being digested. So these are civil borders. These are the military borders that are superimposed over this. But only on the conquered areas do you have governor-generalships. The general should give you a hint of what's really going on. And who's conquered? Finland, Poland, Ukraine, Caucasus, all the khanates of Turkestan, Siberia. And China has also done this of having provincial boundaries that come and go and move around and also superimposing military regions that also move around. Whereas if you look at, like, the United States, boundaries of states, they really haven't changed. You've got one set of states and then that's that. But what's going on here is these boundaries are a mechanism of ingesting, digesting, and if you can't fully digest and turn everybody into a regular province, at least you can neutralize these ethnic groups.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right. So, Mackinder's all about the advantages of internal lines of communication. Well, it comes with a corresponding disadvantage, which is multiple neighbors. And these are Russia's no-kidding security threats circa 1900. And from west to east, they're Britain, Germany, Austria-Hungary, the Ottoman Empire, China, and Japan. This security world is really different from Britain's 360-degree 'you can't get me' moat. Lots of people have come to get Russians over the years. And Russians have fought constantly with Germans, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, the Ottoman Empire. They have suffered catastrophic defeats to the Mongols, the Swedes, initially to Napoleon, the Germans in World War I, and in the Cold War. But each time, the central position allowed them to rise again. In this world, power is a function of land. Neighbors are dangerous, even the itty-bitty ones. If they're unstable, that instability can ooze over the border. Worse yet, if they're strong, stable neighbors and they have an idea that you're going to become a menu item for them, that's even worse. So, in maritime power, the army is for expeditionary warfare. What's the expedition? You cross the ocean and go somewhere else. That's the expedition. Uh-uh, in this world. Purpose number one of an army in this world is you protect the ruling regime. So if you think about the People's Liberation Army, its goal number one, or mission number one, is to keep the Communist Party in power. That's not what the armies are in the maritime world. It's also, secondarily, is garrisoning...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: vampire, prevent defections. And then thirdly, it's for border defense, and as you're fighting at the border, whatever you win you keep, right? As you're expanding outwards, you hope.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: In this world, away games are a rare event. Most of the fighting's occurring right on the border.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So these are statistics about deaths, not just casualties, wounded people. This is dead people. Uh, these are the sunk costs, Lord. No one's breathing life into these people, they're sunk casualties.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So if you're looking at Axis military deaths, you got 3.2 million dead German soldiers, 1.5 million Japanese, 330,000 Italians.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Now let's look at the Allied land powers. 8.5 million dead Russian soldiers, nearly a million dead Poles, 1.3 million dead Chinese, and 340,000 dead Frenchmen.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Okay, look at the maritime powers by some statistics, Britain and the United States. Britain with 326,000 deaths, the United States 295,000. By some statistics, they suffered fewer deaths than Italy or France, and France wasn't in the war that long.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then when you add in all the civilian deaths, the numbers for the continental powers go catastrophic, whereas they hardly budge for the maritime powers. So you've got over 7 million dead Germans, over 25 and a half dead Russians, almost 7 million dead Poles, 11 million dead Chinese, nearly a million dead Frenchmen.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So the deaths for the land powers are measured in millions, whereas the maritime powers, it's hundreds of thousands. Still not a great number. And what's going on here? It's because you're not insulated by the oceans, so the fighting tends to be on home territory. If that's the case, you're ruining civilians at a really rapid clip.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And no country in its right mind wants to fight on the main front if there are alternatives. But this is the continental power's unavoidable agony that when a neighbor invades, you go a choice on that day, either you're going to capitulate or you're going to fight, and you're doing it on the neighbor's timing. That's Ukraine's problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Whereas if you're a maritime power observing someone else being invaded, because in theory, it's pretty tough to invade you by sea, it's a major point of strategy as to whether you're going to intervene at all. And if you are, what is the timing? What instruments of national power are you going to use? And these are things that the United States needs to think about very carefully before it intervenes all over the place and overextends.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But there're advantages, if you win, uh, this, uh, kind of warfare, if you think about Russians, uh, enormous sacrifices I've just listed that for Stalin, it means you dominate Eastern Europe, or Russia does, for the duration of the Cold War. But understand that that kind of territorial domination comes at a huge price. The value of the object had better be worth it before you try it.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: In this world, there are certain rules for playing this game. Rule number one: no two-front wars. I've shown you all the neighbors. If they gang up on you, it will be game over in a very bad way for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Secondly, no great power neighbors. Why? Because today's friend can be tomorrow's foe, and that spells trouble. So what do you do?

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: You take on your neighbors sequentially, right? One at a time. You set them up to fail, you destabilize the rising, you ingest the failing, and you set up buffer zones in between, and you await the opportune moment to pounce and absorb. This is Vladimir Putin's game.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Uh, better yet, you get the neighbors to do the work for you. How? You sow their mutual resentments, you get them to fight each other, you deluge them with fake news, so that Russia can play the role of the jackal state, which is once the neighbors have sufficiently weakened themselves and weaken each other, that Russia can move on in and steal a kill made by others.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: This theory of security has certain problems. One, you're surrounding yourself by failing states. You can look at Russia and China, and they're surrounded by some of the most dysfunctional places on the planet, and you can go, gee whiz, are they unlucky? Or are they complicit? And moreover, there are no enduring alliances in this world, because the neighbors figure it out. The hegemonic power is just a long-term problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And there's also no counsel on when to quit. Like, how much territory do you take before you choke on it? And it turns out that there's been a tendency over the course of China's and Russia's long histories to overextend, and this helps account for the implosions of various empires and dynasties. Now, before you dismiss this security paradigm, understand it was very effective, uh, certainly prior to the Industrial Revolution. No one cared in this world about collateral damage or killing a bunch of innocent people. This paradigm is all about killing people, breaking things, to take territory, and you've seen it operating in real time in your own lives.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: In Syria, Ukraine, and other places. Uh, this is why those ancient ruins, the ones that still remain, are ruinous, are ruins, because this sort of warfare is ruinous. All right, here is, uh, one of the preeminent historians of the late Tsarist period, Vasily Klyuchevsky, who's describing his country.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: He said, the history of Russia is a history of a country in process of colonizing itself. Her area of colonization grows in tandem with her national territory, at times shrinking, at times expanding, age-old movement. Therefore, the periods in our history are the stages which our people have gone through in the occupation and development of the land acquired by them. There is absolutely no mention of the people who actually live on this land. It's like irrelevant from his point of view.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But, uh, this is the way Russians have long looked at things. That if you have small neighbors, uh, someone big may take them over. And if, uh, even worse, if the weak become strong in their own right, that's even worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: It's dangerous, you want to keep moving outwards and expanding.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And here a generation earlier you have Fyodor Dostoevsky, the novelist, the author of Crime and Punishment and other light reads. Here he is confiding in his diary: 'In Europe we are hangers-on and slaves, whereas we shall go to Asia as masters. In Europe we were Asiatics, whereas in Asia we too are Europeans. Our civilizing mission in Asia will bribe our spirit and drive us thither.'

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So this idea of empire is very deeply rooted in Russian thinking. And here is Count Sergey Witte, he's probably the most distinguished public servant in the late Czarist period, he was the finance minister. And he's looking at the world in 1903 and go 'Look, the problem for Russia is to obtain as large a share as possible of the outlived Oriental states' and he's thinking of the Ottoman Empire but particularly of China. And he goes 'Look, Russia historically, geographically has the undisputed right to the lion's share of the expected prey and this elemental movement of Russia began a long time ago with Ivan the Terrible, aptly named, and the absorption by Russia of a considerable portion of the Chinese Empire is only a question of time unless the Chinese get their act together.' So here you have a statesman at the highest levels of the Russian government who is looking at Russian national security, it's all about territorial expansion.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Well, Putin also looks at the world this way apparently. The problem for the likes of Putin is if a continental power botches strategy, its known world can vanish forever.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And this is what happened to Imperial China, Imperial Russia, and many venerable civilizations. I love this photograph of an aristocrat's whimsical palace that's long gone as a result of one of the most horrific genocides of the 20th century, when the communists made good their promise to get rid of entire social classes and I mean get rid of like gone forever, and with the Bolshevik revolution, the ensuing Russian civil war, followed by collectivization, and then the Great Purges that finished the job. This is the continental world. It is a world without insurance policies.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Okay, now for the maritime world. It is a different event. I love this picture, no idea how accurate it is, but I think it illustrates the point. So Spykman is talking about all of these different rimlands and things, and you look at the Atlantic Ocean, it's the center of the world economy in the 19th century and it's all about the rimlands producing all of this wealth, and then you put it on ships and you send it back and forth so that the seas are a commons that you want to share. The whole point is you want to get goods going from port to port. And in our own highly networked age, think about it, the seas are the original network that potentially connected everyone to everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, the genesis of this maritime world, I maybe it goes back further...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: At least as far back as I go is the Athenian Empire that is hugging the shores of the Aegean and Ionian Seas and it's all about the trade that's accruing there that's paying for their empire.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: The Romans likewise, another rimland empire, very different from the consolidated empires like Russia and China.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And think about the terminology: Mediterranean, 'medi' middle, 'terrane' land, so it's the sea in the middle of the lands.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Whereas the terminology for China, Zhongguo, 'zhong's' central, 'guo' kingdom, so it's the kingdom among the kingdoms.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: One empire term emphasizes the centrality of the sea, the other the centrality of the land. Different way of looking at things.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And here's the Byzantine Empire, another rimland empire. And then for a while no one controls the rimlands anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And in part what's going on is there's always been a lot of money to be made on east-west trade. It used to go by camels among other things on the Silk Route, but whoever controlled the westward toll booth on that thing made loads of money.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Because that's where it splits, right? And it's going to go on the southern or northern shores of the Mediterranean. A lot of fighting over that piece of territory.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: For a while, the Muslim conquest settled it, they controlled it. And then while those things are going on, there is continental consolidation going on in Europe.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And both modern Germany and modern France trace their origins to the empire created by Charlemagne at the turn of the eighth and ninth centuries.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then if you go to the end of the first millennium, you look at Europe, no one controls the rimlands, but you've got these large states that things have coalesced into.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then the Crusades in part are a fight of these larger powers in Europe to try to come back and get that toll booth back again, which they fail. Instead the Ottomans have it for a long time.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And this is when the Europeans have to get clever because they want to trade with Asia. So they, 'Aha! we're going to go by ship and we'll go the long way around.' And it's the Spanish and the Portuguese that try this, except they bump into the New World.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: New to them, not so much to other people. And they also go, 'Ooh, a lot of gold and silver here.' And so they forget about the spices, silk, and the high-end dinnerware and they're going to do heavy metal instead.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: However, it's the Dutch who are the real followers of maritime empire, which is very much defined by their trading bases. Their problem is their location in Europe is vulnerable.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And it's not surprising that it is a member of the Dutch Republic, Hugo Grotius, who's the founding father of international law.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Why? Because maritime empires want to be able to use the seas as commons, don't want people pirating their trade, and would like everyone to have rules so that everyone can trade in safety.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So here's Hugo Grotius in his Mare Liberum, 'Freedom of the Seas', writing that every nation is free to travel to every other nation and trade with it.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then in his Law of War and Peace, he cites a Roman jurist, so this is going way back.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: To all men belong the use of the sea, and then he quotes a Byzantine compilation of Roman law—i.e., going way back in Western thinking—'By natural law the following are common to everyone: the air, flowing water, the sea, and in consequence the seashore.' Well, this is very much a product of Western civilization, but not necessarily shared by others.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: For instance, in 1996, China signed the UN—ratified—the UN Convention of the Law of the Seas, UNCLOS. And, except China says that freedom of navigation does not extend within 200 nautical miles of their coastline, except under UNCLOS the number's 12 nautical miles, and in the East and South China Seas, these numbers make a difference.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right. The British are the real pioneers of maritime empire because they don't suffer the Dutch problem—that Napoleon eventually takes care of the Dutch and integrates them into the French empire—but the Britons have this 360-degree moat. They're the only power in Europe that doesn't need to maintain a large standing army, because if Britain could maintain a competent navy, no army's going to reach British shores because they're going to be drowned at sea.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So the Britain could take a weakness—its dependence on trade—and turn it into a strength. So the trade is going to finance the navy that's going to protect both the British homeland and some of the trade. And then Britain is going to be compounding wealth while its neighbors are busy—A, they, its neighbors have to fund large armies that can be economy-killing and also can be coup-generating, that are going to be constantly fighting with each other and destroying wealth in the process.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So while a navy could provide Britain with a prevent-defeat strategy—i.e., no one's going to get to Britain—but it couldn't—a navy's insufficient for a deliver-victory strategy. It's not going to eliminate these continental problems. But if Britain could keep compounding wealth over time and then its neighbors are busy destroying wealth at a rapid clip, then the difference in wealth is going to get larger and in that sense things will get better for Britain.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And the Britons, after much trial and error, came up with a way of integrating multiple instruments of national power to deal with this gentleman on the horse. Oh, by the way, if you want to see the horse's skeleton you can go to the British Army Museum in London, but that's another story. Anyway, gross story. But anyhow, here's good old Napoleon. And the Britons tried different things and eventually figured out the problem is that navies are rarely decisive in wartime. You've got to have other instruments to integrate. Armies sometimes can be. And so this was where the Britons come up with grand strategy. They coined the term because they need to do it. And here's their problem on the eve of the Napoleonic Wars. Europe has a bunch of consolidated...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: You start with the states east and west, and then there's this big tidbit zone in the middle. It's called the Holy Roman Empire, but it's really a tidbit zone. And Napoleon eats up the tidbits, and then you get to 1812 and this is the maximum extent of the French Empire. And this is when the guru of Western warfare, Carl von Clausewitz, is coming up with his ideas about ground warfare. It's a really bad year for everybody—not for Napoleon, but everybody else. And this is when the Britons are thinking about, how are you going to use maritime power to deal with this?

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And here's what they come up with: their strategy for elephant hunting. How do you deal with a continental power that's trying to upend the trading system on which you're making money? So, rule number one is you keep the home economy growing. That is number one, because that's going to produce the money that's going to fund your military, fund your allies, allow you to do everything. Secondly, don't let the elephant forage. You want to close down enemy trade through blockade, commerce raiding, so you're going to throw it back on its own dwindling resources and its increasingly embittered allies.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Rule number three, you want to rent an elephant. You want to find the continental power that is most directly threatened by your continental problem, and you want to arm, fund, do whatever you can to keep them in the fight, because that's the main front of whatever this war is.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Rule number four, Britain needs to find a theater that is peripheral to that main theater, where access by sea is much more efficient than access by land, and you want to fight there. Why? Because it's going to do several things for you. The attrition between the peripheral theater and the main theater's going to add up. That's good. You're going to force the enemy to fight with divided attention and ideally, if it's a really good theater, you're going to relieve some of the pressure on the main front as your enemy's going to have to divert resources.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Rule number five, a rule that Britons broke at great cost in World War I: do not take on the enemy main force directly. You certainly do not do that as an opening move. Think about it. A continental power's major strength is its army. That is not Britain's great strength. Britain's great strength is its navy, its ability to generate wealth, and therefore its ability to survive protracted war. Don't play the continental game. It won't be good for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And rule number six, if you're going to fight on the main front, only do it after you have really bled that elephant. So it has been gravely weakened, and you only do it with lots of friends, gang up on them.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: As much as a continental power might want to play this game, it cannot. Why? One is insufficient maritime access. When Mahan talked about egress to the sea, basically they're living there on these narrow seas that are blockaded, which means you cannot get your surface fleet out reliably in wartime. And it means your merchant marine is going nowhere. And in addition, the—

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Continental problems often have a surfeit of neighbors, some of which might invade it, so it can't play this game. But if Britain did and kept at it, it could set itself and its enemies on opposing trend lines. So the war is going to increasingly encroach on enemy territory with the main theater and the more peripheral theaters, which is going to undermine the enemy economy, productive capacity, morale and together this is going to undermine the ability for your enemy to continue fighting.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: In effect, it's a strategy of protracted war, but Britain's going to win by exhausting the other guy first. And if you think about it, the focus isn't primarily on the military instrument of national power. It's actually much more about economics, coalitions, institutions, but they're prerequisites for it. You can't play it if you do not have sanctuary at home. Sorry about the double negative, but the point is, if people are going to invade over your borders or level your industrial base, you have a hard time trying to do this.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Moreover, it requires access, access to peripheral theaters, access to your alliance partners, access to overseas markets. And it also requires institutional stability at home in order to follow a consistent strategy over a long period of time. These are some of the characteristics that Mahan would later emphasize.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, after the Napoleonic wars, there followed a century of unprecedented prosperity in Europe where there was no continent-wide war again. During this period there were some smaller wars and it meant that people were just making a lot of money playing the maritime game, which is just trade according to the rules, compound wealth, don't level each other's buildings, you'll make money. But World War I came, never mind.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right, now I'm into the second distinguishing characteristic between maritime and continental powers. The first one was the ability or inability to defend at sea. Here's the second one. Continental powers face contiguous threats, so they have to focus on national security and often they want to insulate themselves from neighbors and they want exclusive zones to do this, and they often want those exclusive zones to extend out into the seas around them.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Maritime powers think this is crazy land because they've got the relative security of the moat, no one's going to invade them. So they're going to focus on national prosperity and trade is the path to prosperity and to security because this is the money that's going to pay for everything. So they want maritime access to markets. So they want to operate with the oceans being commons and they want it to go all the way up from port to port because you can't trade unless you go port to port.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So from these distinguishing characteristics, you get two mutually exclusive world orders out of this. One of them is we're going to divide the world up into very large spheres of influence, each a world unto itself and the other one is: No, no, no, we're going to operate under universal rules, and the seas are going to be open to everyone. All right, the

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: The industrial revolution upends empires of both types, and it's going to usher in a maritime rules-based global order.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And it's a game-changer because it introduces compounded economic growth, which is indeed revolutionary, and it arises over a combination, not just of technological changes, but also institutional changes.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: What are institutions? It's how we organize each other, right? We join into some larger institution, and then it has some purpose in organizing people to do things more efficiently.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So, in the early phases, it's starting with steam power, iron industry, textiles, insurance, and banking. You can't do the trade without insurance and banking for your loans and things—institutions.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And in its later phases, it's about railways, telegraphs, steamships, mass markets, trade, general staffs, armaments. And what's going on is the currency of power is changed.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Power was a function of land back in the day. Why? Because land is where you get the commodities that you can sell, and also this is where you're going to—in rural areas—is where you're going to get your peasant conscripts to field your mass armies.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Well, once wealth is a function of commerce, industry, and trade, it isn't land anymore, and this upends the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: If you think about the world today, who's rich, who's poor, who's powerful, who's not—it's often the degree to which the country is industrialized.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And the industrial revolution opens up the beginnings of a global trading order where you're trying to, through trade negotiations, to minimize transaction costs so everybody can make a lot of money.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And this is a process that is still ongoing in our own day; trade negotiations continue eternally. But it upends the traditional balance of power, the traditional societies, their paradigms—security paradigms—no longer work.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And China, back in the 19th century, was opposed to this incoming maritime order. In our own day, ISIS or whatever is left of it, absolutely reviles the liberal economies and societies that are favoring this sort of trading order.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: If you look at the big yellow area that Mackinder cared about with railways, because railways are a product of the industrial revolution, he was thinking all in terms of an integrated transportation grid there.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Another way of looking at this map is the Roman, British, and Mongol empires all share a piece of real estate—it would be the western terminus of the Silk Road.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: made on the going on on the Silk Road because once the canal is built, even with steamships but the the canal even more so, it is so much cheaper to send things by ship than to try to walk it over Eurasia with draft animals. And it just upends the economics of the continental world versus the maritime world.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So, in the continental world, that Silk Road is a really big deal, and that is prime real estate. However, another piece of the industrial revolution is the Suez Canal, which is going to wreck the camel trade.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And uh let's fast forward 100 years to the 1967 war where you've got Israel fighting Egypt, Jordan and Syria and as part of Egypt's strategy doesn't want Israel using the Suez Canal so they sink a bunch of block ships there. So that's the operational effect: Israeli's not going to be using Suez Canal.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But here is the strategic effect, the ones that actually count in this life. If you look at before the war, um, most what is it, almost 90% of ships were 50,000 deadweight tons or less - and this is numbers of ships, right? And so they could all make it through the Suez Canal, which could take ships up to that size. But then the Suez Canal is closed down from 1967 to 1972-ish, and so that's a long time in world trade. Well, look at the adjustments that take place. All of this, by the time you get later, you get, by '72 you've got almost 30% of the number of ships - not the capacity - are these huge ships that never had existed before. They're not going to make it through the Suez Canal. The Suez Canal has since been widened, but still the biggest ships can't make it through. And so then if you look at the cost of sending oil from the Persian Gulf to Rotterdam in the Netherlands, if you're going to send it in an itty-bitty ship through the canal it might be a little over 13 dollars per ton to send it, whereas if you're sending in a big ship the long way around Africa, I believe it's a third that price.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: So, um strategic effect of things, unexpected. That overhead costs in shipping is is the size of ship gets over a lot of it. It's not about distance.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And here's the other guy who really revolutionized maritime trade, Malcolm McLean, who I bet you've never heard of, but now I'm going to fix that. He ran a trucking company and he decided to put the chassis- his trucks minus the chassis - on ships, what we call containers, above decks, below decks. And when he did this, he reduced the loading costs from nearly 6 dollars a ton to less than 20 cents. And then port time is again much reduced, so ships are out about making money.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And then for those of you who are skeptical about international organizations, here's one you've never heard of, the International Organization for Standardization, then standardized these container sizes to fit one if by truck, two if by railway cars, and thousands if by sea. And this just plummets transport costs. So before you dismiss international organizations, understand that here's a little one you've never heard of.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: States made massive changes in your lives. Okay, and it leads to plummeting transport costs through the duration of the 20th century. And our latest dictator for life, Xi Jinping, he's got this Belt Road thing that he thinks is going to be a good idea. Uh, good luck with that one. And here are the statistics to back me up.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Until 1960, the average tanker size was 20,000 deadweight tons. And if you look in our own day, the smallest ultra large crude tankers are a quarter of a million deadweight tons. I believe that the longest train can take maybe 600 containers, on a good day, whereas the largest container ships can take over 21,000 with cargoes valued at over 1 billion dollars. So, it is so much cheaper to send things by sea. And, oh, on the Belt Road thing, it would be nice if the whole thing were continuous, it's not. It would also be nice if they were all the same gauge, it's not. So you're loading, unloading, loading, unloading, what a mess.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Also, not only is it far cheaper to send things by sea, it is far more secure. If you're going to run the Belt Road thing, or it's the belt part of it, the land part, you've got to control the whole thing end to end. You're going through some of the most unstable places there are. Good luck with that one. Whereas in the maritime world, if it is unstable or they do blockships and close down the Suez Canal, well forget it, just go the long way around, it'll be fine.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And also for China with its geographical setup as it is, the seas are going to be open to it only in peacetime. In wartime, this has nothing to do with anyone doing anything to them, it's just facts. It is surrounded by loads of neighbors, shallow seas, lots of islands that are completely blocked in from the high seas and from very predictable points. In wartime, those places become kill zones. Merchant traffic cannot make it through those places unless you're friendly with everybody, which China currently isn't, maybe it'll change. And even your surface ships won't make it out. So peace is definitely the better thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: If you look at the end of the last Cold War, to sadly we're into the second one, but look at how world trade just takes off. It was the miracle of our lifetimes. It lifted hundreds of millions of people out of poverty. The problem for the likes of Putin is that this wealth-producing trade was not happening at his house, but the houses of those invested in the trading order, not in extortion. But with the second Cold War we're into more inefficient things again. All right, this brings me to the third distinguishing characteristics of continental and maritime powers, and it has to do with the transportation revolution. So continental powers...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Historically have leveraged interior lines of communication that allow them to garrison their empire, deploy their armies against neighbors, and then to form a contiguous alliance system.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And that worked well when uh power was a function of land.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Well, that's not true anymore. The maritime powers uh are using these exterior lines of communications, the ones by sea, to give them access not just to the people right next to them, but to the entire globe, which opens the possibility for them to form a global alliance system. And from this reliance on interior versus uh exterior lines of communication, it gives you the possibility of what sort of alliance systems that you can have.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: All right. The industrial revolution put continental empires on notice, not simply because land transport is so much uh more expensive than sea transport, but but because it opened the possibility of a positive sum world order. The land empire MO of beating up all your neighbors, well, it's negative sum. What do I mean by that? Is you're fighting over territory, you're damaging the goods while you're fighting. So whatever someone lost in pristine territory now looks like this. And uh it's negative sum. You're destroying wealth at a very rapid clip. Whereas the maritime world is, 'No, boys, don't do it that way.' You want to have trading partners, you want to have win-win so everybody makes money so we have compounded growth.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Uh and this maritime uh order is focusing on freedom of navigation, free trade, international laws and institutions that facilitate trade in order to minimize transaction costs so we can maximize the money. And the insurance system for all of this is alliance system. So most countries aren't maritime by geography, but if they all gang up together and they coordinate diverse instruments of national power when they're dealing with these rogue continental states that want to upend the trading system, they can turn out in force to protect the rules that protect them all.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And here's the ultimate insurance policy that was created by the greatest generation, which were the conscripts of World War I, the people who were told to go up and over trenches and to see how that would go. And the ones who survived came back and tried to raise their families during the Great Depression. And then by the time they were of the age to be strategic leaders, um they're off sending their own kids to fight World War II. And they concluded, and this is uh this generation is in Europe and the United States both, they concluded that the solution to Great Depressions and World Wars was institution building on a global scale. And they built institutions that have lasted to the present and held the peace until in the industrialized world until Vladimir Putin decided to work his magic. But they create the UN, the IMF, NATO, the predecessor...

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: successor institutions of the World Trade Organization and also of the the European Union. And their idea is we must hash out our differences with diplomats and lawyers because when you send the soldiers in it is whatever gains you think you're going to get are going to be inferior to what you would have done with the diplomats. It's it's too ruinous fighting each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right. So, if you look at where the fighting that did go on in the Cold War because it was cold in the industrialized world but it was really hot elsewhere. A lot of it is on this Mackinder's inner marginal crescent which is where the maritime and continental worlds hit. So if there's civil wars going in there, the one side of the Cold War wants to put its finger on the scale and the other one wants to put its finger on the scale and there we go. And that is all wealth destroying and helps explain why growth rates and things weren't as or globally had problems because you're destroying wealth.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: All right. The maritime world is invisible. And this took me 15 years teaching at the War College to notice the obvious. Sometimes the obvious becomes obvious by stating it and let's see if it works for you. The continental world's visible because it's about positive objectives. It's all about making things happen. So you want to take territory, you can see it. Either you did or you didn't. But the maritime world is about negative objectives. It's preventing things from bad things from happening. And you can never prove that you prevented anything. Maybe no one was trying anything to begin with. And it's invisible, right? The thing you prevented never happened, surprise surprise you can't see it.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But in the continental world, it's all about positive operational objectives. You're destroying neighbors to take their territory but at the strategic level, you are destroying wealth at a massive clip. That is a problem. Whereas the maritime world, it's invisible of all the bad things that you're preventing anyone messing with the international trading order which you're making your money from in part, a lot of domestic activity as well. And navies, their missions more often than not are also about negative objectives. Not all of them.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: What are they? And think about naval missions. Most of them happen in peacetime. Yeah, wartime happens too but for navies it's mostly in peacetime. And what are they up to? Mission number one is prevent the destruction of the global trading system. That would be important. Your prosperity hinges on that one. The second really important mission is prevent limits on freedom of navigation because if you cannot get goods to port to port, it's pretty useless. And thirdly, it is to deter expansion into taking over big countries taking over little countries. Think about it, the bedrock principle of international law is sovereignty. If we're all going to be invading each other, what international law is there? And the antidotes to rogue behavior in this maritime world are—

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Alliances, diplomacy, sanctions, embargoes, containment, numerous instruments of national power and navies are about deterrence, blockade and commerce raiding. What you're doing is you're preventing the continental problem, the elephant, from foraging. You're just saying you're getting a timeout from the rules-based order because you can't behave yourself. And you can go, well, the continental problem's still there. Well, what's interesting...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So you all know about compound growth, it's powerful, right? Over generations. What people don't think about is the effect of negative objectives. Sanctions are like economic chemotherapy. What you're doing is preventing 1 or 2% growth per annum, even with leaky sanctions, right? Well, and you go, so what? Well, oh no, so very what is the difference over several generations is the difference between North and South Korea. I get it, North Korea's still there, but they've got a much smaller piggy bank than they would have otherwise.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And yeah, you're not getting rid of the problem, but if the rogue state has nuclear weapons and you go, oh gee, we just want to get rid of it. Really you want to have a nuclear war to get rid of North Korea? Isn't this world is not about the operational win of totally eliminating a problem. It's rather, it's contained at acceptable cost. So the North Koreans want to live miserable lives at home, have fun with it, go live miserable lives at home. And uh, it's being contained at acceptable cost. That's what's going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay. The optimist at the end of the Cold War thought surely everyone wants to join the maritime party. Not so. The continental maritime difference of opinion continues and I guess you like the slide here of Mr. Fit and Misfit, Vladimir Putin and Kim Jong-un. They want to upend this maritime order. They want to hollow out international institutions, upend international law, kill off our alliance system so we can return the world into warring spheres of influence and that we can live the sort of squalid lives their citizens have to live.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, to summarize. Uh, there are very different geopolitics associated with continental and maritime powers. And these discriminating factors are the inability or ability to defend primarily at sea, a focus on insulation from versus access to the world and our reliance on interior versus exterior lines of communication. And these accidents of geography have predisposed different preferences. One, a continental power typically looks at territory as something you might want to take. Whereas a maritime power's no, no, no, that's a market, I want to make money there. And who wants to own their domestic problems? Eww. And then there are uh, the continental desire for exclusive zones whereas a maritime's saying, no, we want commons, we want to share the seas, space and now the internet.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: cyber and the air that this is the way to do stuff. And then many people noticed that most countries, except for a few islands, uh they aren't strictly maritime or or if they're completely landlocked, I mean, most countries are not strictly maritime or continental by geography.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But there's a corollary. Alliances can bestow a collective maritime position and power package to those countries that join in if it gets large enough that even though they themselves are not maritime alone, but together they can be.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And this choice about which way to go is very financially consequential in our own day, uh because typically continentalists tend to be dictators, not always, but they typically, and they're all about hemorrhaging cash to dominate their own citizens and their neighbors, whereas the maritime powers are like, 'That's nuts. Why would you ever do that?' And they're keeping the growth compounding.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And if you think about China, it has benefited more than any other country from rejoining the family of nations and this maritime world under Deng Xiaoping.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: But this, the legacy of history and the, the burden of history, uh, these continentalist outlooks like, 'I need to take Taiwan, really?' um, weigh heavily, and the choice is consequential.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: And there is only one win-win solution. It's however flawed this international order is, it's a work in progress, and uh, it's the only win-win solution is to deploy the diplomats and lawyers to hash out these things in international forums.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Because if we're all going to send soldiers, we're going to get a Third World War with nuclear follow-on effects. And, uh, we'll see whether humanity makes it if this is the, it's tremendously consequential, particularly at this juncture in history.

full_transcript.speaker: Sarah Paine
full_transcript.text: Anyway, thank you for your attention, and uh, I appreciate you all coming.

highlights: The fundamental divide in geopolitics is between continental powers, which prioritize land-based territorial security and insulation from neighbors, and maritime powers, which focus on national prosperity through global trade access.

The Industrial Revolution shifted the primary source of power from land ownership to commercial industry, favoring a maritime-led order that compounds wealth through low-cost sea transportation and global networks.

Maritime strategy often focuses on "negative objectives"—invisible successes where catastrophic events, such as the disruption of global trade or the invasion of sovereign states, are prevented through deterrence and alliances.

While continental warfare is a negative-sum game that destroys wealth to gain territory, the maritime rules-based order offers a positive-sum alternative where international institutions and law minimize transaction costs for all participants.

Although few nations are maritime by geography alone, modern alliances allow diverse countries to collectively project maritime power, protecting the global commons and the flow of commerce that sustains their economies.
summary:
Sarah Paine explores the fundamental geopolitical divide between continental powers ("elephants") and maritime powers ("whales"), arguing that geography dictates how empires organize the world. Continental powers, such as Russia and China, are defined by their inability to defend themselves at sea, leading to a focus on massive standing armies, territorial expansion, and the creation of exclusive "spheres of influence" to manage threats from neighbors. In contrast, maritime powers like Great Britain and the United States leverage geographic "moats" to focus on national prosperity through trade. These powers treat the seas as a "commons" and utilize "exterior lines of communication" to build global alliances, favoring international law and maritime access over the physical conquest of territory.

The transcript emphasizes how the Industrial Revolution shifted the currency of power from land to commerce, enabling a "positive-sum" maritime order. Paine highlights that while continental strategy is "visible" and often destructive—seizing land at the cost of immense human life and wealth—maritime strategy is often "invisible," focusing on "negative objectives" like preventing disruptions to global trade. Through the use of blockades, sanctions, and alliance-building, maritime powers aim to contain "rogue" continental states without resorting to direct, ruinous land wars. Ultimately, Paine argues that the modern rules-based order, supported by international institutions, remains the only sustainable path for global wealth and peace, whereas a return to continentalist expansionism risks catastrophic, negative-sum conflict.
description:
In this lecture, military historian Sarah Paine lays out the geopolitical framework that has shaped global power for two thousand years: the divide between continental empires that pursue security through territorial conquest (China, Russia) and maritime powers that pursue prosperity through trade (Britain, the US). Recorded first in Paine's 2025 series, this is the framework that all her other lectures build on.

00:00:00 - Setting the stage
00:12:10 - The continental powers
00:29:12 - The maritime alternative
00:42:00 - How the Industrial Revolution changed everything
00:52:00 - Why Putin wants to break the world
topics: The geopolitical distinction between continental and maritime powers
Classic geopolitical theories including Mahan’s sea power and Mackinder’s heartland
The historical development and security strategies of Russia and China
How the Industrial Revolution and containerization transformed global trade
The development of the rules-based international order and global alliance systems
